problems.title: Eliminate the constants
problems.problem: Eliminate $a, b$ and $c$ from $z = a(x + y) + b(x - y) + abt + c$.
problems.hint: • Differentiate to eliminate $a,b$ and $c$ and get a PDE.
problems.solution:
The given equation is \begin{equation}\label{eq:02Jan2026-1} z = a(x + y) + b(x - y) + abt + c. \end{equation} Differentiate \eqref{eq:02Jan2026-1} partially with respect to $x,y$ and $t$, respectively, to get \begin{align*} \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} & = a + b \\ \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} & = a - b \\ \frac{\partial z}{\partial t} & = ab. \end{align*}
Since, \begin{align*} (a+b)^2 + (a-b)^2 = 4ab \implies \left( \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} \right) ^2+ \left( \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} \right) ^2 = 4\left( \frac{\partial z}{\partial t} \right). \end{align*}

problems.title: Bernoulli differential equation
problems.problem:
Solve the differential equation 
    \[
        (x^3 y^2 + xy) \mathrm{d} x = \mathrm{d} y.
    \]
problems.hint: • This is Bernoulli's equation.
problems.solution:
Re-write the given equation as \begin{equation}\label{eq:09Jan2026-1} \frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x} = x^3 y^2 + xy \implies \frac{1}{y^2} \frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x} - x^3 = \frac{x}{y}. \end{equation} Put \[ v = \frac{1}{y} \implies \frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}x} = -\frac{1}{y^2}\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x} . \] Thus, \eqref{eq:09Jan2026-1} reduces to \begin{equation}\label{eq:09Jan2026-2} -\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}x} - x^3 = xv \implies \frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}x} + xv = - x^3, \end{equation} which is a linear ODE and can be solved by multiplying with an integrating factor.
The integrating factor is \begin{align*} e^{\int x \mathrm{d} x} = e^{x^2 / 2}. \end{align*} Multiplication of the integrating factor to \eqref{eq:09Jan2026-2} gives \begin{align*} & \frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}x} e^{x^2 / 2} + xv e^{x^2 / 2} = - x^3 e^{x^2 / 2} \\ \implies & \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}x} \left( v e^{x^2 / 2} \right) = -x^3 e^{x^2 / 2} \\ \implies & v e^{x^2 / 2} = -\int x^3 e^{x^2 / 2} \\ \implies & v e^{x^2 / 2} = - 2e^{x^2 / 2} \left( \frac{x^2}{2} - 1 \right) + c \\ \implies & \frac{1}{y} e^{x^2 / 2} = - 2e^{x^2 / 2} \left( \frac{x^2}{2} - 1 \right) + c \\ \implies & \frac{1}{y} = - 2\left( \frac{x^2}{2} - 1 \right) + c e^{-x^2 / 2} \\ \implies & \frac{1}{y} = (2 - x^2) + c e^{-x^2 / 2} . \end{align*}

problems.title: Population Growth with Rate Proportional to Population
problems.problem: If the population of a country doubles in 50 years, in how many years will it triple under the assumption that the rate of increase is proportional to the number of inhabitants.
problems.hint:
• Let $P(t)$ denote the population at time $t$. Then, $\frac{\mathrm{d}P}{\mathrm{d}t} \propto P$. Translate into a differential equation involving $P(t)$.
  • Solve this differential equation to express $P(t)$, then use the given doubling condition to determine the constant and find when $P(t)$ becomes three times its initial value.
problems.solution:
Let $P(t)$ denote the population at time $t$. Since the rate of increase of the population is proportional to the number of inhabitants, we have \[ \frac{dP}{dt} = kP, \] where $k>0$ is a constant of proportionality.
Separating variables and integrating, \begin{align*} \frac{1}{P}\,dP = k\,dt & \implies \ln P = kt + C \\ & \implies P(t) = Ce^{kt}. \end{align*} Let $P(0)=P_0$, so $C=P_0$ and \[ P(t) = P_0 e^{kt}. \]
We are given that the population doubles in 50 years: \begin{align*} & P(50) = 2P_0 = P_0 e^{50k} \\ \implies & e^{50k} = 2 \implies k = \frac{\ln 2}{50}. \end{align*}
To find the time $t$ when the population triples, we set \[ P(t) = 3P_0 = P_0 e^{kt}. \] This gives \[ e^{kt} = 3 \quad \implies \quad t = \frac{\ln 3}{k} = 50\frac{\ln 3}{\ln 2}. \] Hence, the population will triple in \[ \boxed{50\frac{\ln 3}{\ln 2} \approx 79 \text{ years}.} \]

problems.title: An exact differential equation (3x^2 + 4xy) \mathrm{d} x + (2x^2 + 2y) \mathrm{d} y= 0
problems.problem:
Solve the differential equation
    \[
        (3x^2 + 4xy) \mathrm{d} x + (2x^2 + 2y) \mathrm{d} y= 0.
    \]
problems.hint: • This is an exact differential equation.
problems.solution:
The given differential equation is \begin{equation}\label{eq:23Jan2026-1} (3x^2 + 4xy) \mathrm{d} x + (2x^2 + 2y) \mathrm{d} y= 0. \end{equation} We will first check if the equation \eqref{eq:23Jan2026-1} is exact or not. Let $M = 3x^2 + 4xy$ and $N = 2x^2 + 2y$. Note that \begin{align*} \frac{\partial M}{\partial y} = 4x \text{ and } \frac{\partial N}{\partial x} = 4x. \end{align*} Since $\frac{\partial M}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial N}{\partial x} $ in the full domain, the equation is exact.
As the equation is exact, we must find $F$ such that \begin{align*} & \frac{\partial F(x,y)}{\partial x} = M(x,y) = 3x^2 + 4xy \text{ and } \\ & \frac{\partial F(x,y)}{\partial y} = N(x,y) = 2x^2 + 2y. \end{align*} From the first equation, \begin{align*} F(x,y) & = \int M(x,y) \mathrm{d} x + \phi(y) \\ & = \int (3x^2 + 4xy) \mathrm{d} x + \phi (y) \\ & = x^3 + 2x^2 y + \phi (y). \end{align*} Since \begin{align*} \frac{\partial F(x,y)}{\partial y} = N(x,y) & \implies 2x^2 + \frac{\mathrm{d}\phi (y)}{\mathrm{d}y} = 2x^2 + 2y \\ & \implies \frac{\mathrm{d}\phi (y)}{\mathrm{d}y} = 2y \\ & \implies \phi (y) = \int 2y \mathrm{d} y + c = y^2 + c, \end{align*} where $c$ is an arbitrary constant. Thus, \[ F(x,y) = x^3 + 2x^2 y + y^2 + c. \]

problems.title: Solve p + 3q = 5z + \tan (y - 3x)
problems.problem:
Solve 
    \[
        p + 3q = 5z + \tan (y - 3x),
    \]
    where $p = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x}$ and $q = \frac{\partial z}{\partial y}. $
problems.hint: • Use Lagrange equation, that is, solve \[ \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{1} = \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{3} = \frac{\mathrm{d} z}{5z + \tan (y - 3x)}. \]
problems.solution:
We will use Lagrange equation, that is, solve \begin{equation}\label{eq:30Jan2026-1} \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{1} = \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{3} = \frac{\mathrm{d} z}{5z + \tan (y - 3x)}. \end{equation} Taking the first two factor of \eqref{eq:30Jan2026-1}, we get \begin{align*} \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{1} = \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{3} & \implies \mathrm{d} y - 3 \mathrm{d} x = 0 \\ & \implies y - 3x = c_1, \end{align*} where $c_1$ is an arbitrary constant.
Now look at the \eqref{eq:30Jan2026-1} again and put $y - 3x = c_1$. Consider the first and the last factors, we get \begin{align*} \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{1} = \frac{\mathrm{d} z}{5z + \tan c_1} & \implies \mathrm{d} x - \frac{\mathrm{d} z}{5z + \tan c_1} = 0 \\ & \implies x - \frac{1}{5} \ln (5z + \tan c_1) = c_2' \\ & \implies 5x - \ln (5z + \tan c_1) = 5c_2' = c_2. \end{align*}
Thus, the final solution of \eqref{eq:30Jan2026-1} will be \[ 5x - \ln (5z + \tan (y - 3x)) = \phi (y - 3x), \] where $\phi $ is an arbitrary function.

problems.title: Solve p + 3q = 5z + \tan (y - 3x)
problems.problem:
Solve 
    \[
        p + 3q = 5z + \tan (y - 3x),
    \]
    where $p = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x}$ and $q = \frac{\partial z}{\partial y}. $
problems.hint: • Use Lagrange equation, that is, solve \[ \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{1} = \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{3} = \frac{\mathrm{d} z}{5z + \tan (y - 3x)}. \]
problems.solution:
We will use Lagrange equation, that is, solve \begin{equation}\label{eq:30Jan2026-1} \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{1} = \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{3} = \frac{\mathrm{d} z}{5z + \tan (y - 3x)}. \end{equation} Taking the first two factor of \eqref{eq:30Jan2026-1}, we get \begin{align*} \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{1} = \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{3} & \implies \mathrm{d} y - 3 \mathrm{d} x = 0 \\ & \implies y - 3x = c_1, \end{align*} where $c_1$ is an arbitrary constant.
Now look at the \eqref{eq:30Jan2026-1} again and put $y - 3x = c_1$. Consider the first and the last factors, we get \begin{align*} \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{1} = \frac{\mathrm{d} z}{5z + \tan c_1} & \implies \mathrm{d} x - \frac{\mathrm{d} z}{5z + \tan c_1} = 0 \\ & \implies x - \frac{1}{5} \ln (5z + \tan c_1) = c_2' \\ & \implies 5x - \ln (5z + \tan c_1) = 5c_2' = c_2. \end{align*}
Thus, the final solution of \eqref{eq:30Jan2026-1} will be \[ 5x - \ln (5z + \tan (y - 3x)) = \phi (y - 3x), \] where $\phi $ is an arbitrary function.

problems.title: Solving ODE with Laplace transformation
problems.hint: • For any function $f$, \[ \mathcal{L} \left( f^{(n)}(t) \right) = s^n \mathcal{L} (f(t)) - \sum_{i=1}^{n} s^{n-i} f^{(i-1)}(0) \]
problems.solution:
Let $\mathcal{L} (f)$ denotes the Laplace transformation of $f$. Recall that \begin{align*} \mathcal{L} \left( f^{(n)}(t) \right) & = s^n \mathcal{L} (f(t)) - \sum_{i=1}^{n} s^{n-i} f^{(i-1)}(0). \end{align*} Let $\mathcal{L} (x(t)) = X(s)$. Apply the Laplace transformation on the given differential equation, we get \begin{align*} & \mathcal{L} (x'' + 4x) = \mathcal{L} (\sin 3t) \\ \implies & s^2 \mathcal{L} (x(t)) - s x'(0) - s^2 x(0) + 4 \mathcal{L} (x(t)) = \frac{3}{s^2 + 3^2} \\ \implies & s^2 \times X(s) + 4 \mathcal{L} (x(t)) = \frac{3}{s^2 + 9} \\ \implies & (s^2 + 4) X(s) = \frac{3}{s^2 + 9} \\ \implies & X(s) = \frac{3}{(s^2 + 9)(s^2 + 4)}. \end{align*}
Use partial fraction, we get \[ \frac{3}{(s^2 + 9)(s^2 + 4)} = \frac{3}{10}\left( \frac{2}{s^2 + 4} \right) - \frac{1}{5}\left( \frac{3}{x^2 + 9} \right). \] Finding the inverse Laplace transformation gives, \begin{align*} & \mathcal{L} ^{-1} (X(s)) = \mathcal{L} ^{-1} \left( \frac{3}{10}\left( \frac{2}{s^2 + 4} \right) - \frac{1}{5}\left( \frac{3}{x^2 + 9} \right) \right) \\ \implies & x(t) = \mathcal{L} ^{-1} \left( \frac{3}{10}\left( \frac{2}{s^2 + 4} \right) \right) - \mathcal{L} ^{-1} \left( \frac{1}{5} \left( \frac{3}{x^2 + 9} \right) \right) \\ \implies & x(t) = \frac{3}{10} \mathcal{L} ^{-1} \left( \frac{2}{s^2 + 4} \right) - \frac{1}{5} \mathcal{L} ^{-1} \left( \frac{3}{x^2 + 9} \right) \\ \implies & x(t) = \frac{3}{10} \sin 2t - \frac{1}{5} \sin 3t. \end{align*} Thus, \[ x(t) = \frac{3}{10} \sin 2t - \frac{1}{5} \sin 3t. \]

problems.title: Solve the PDE (y^2 + z^2 - x^2)p - 2xy pq = -2xz
problems.problem:
Solve the following partial differential equation:
    \[
        (y^2 + z^2 - x^2)p - 2xy pq = -2xz,
    \]
    where $p = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} $ and $q = \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} $.
problems.hint:
• Write the equation in Lagrange's form $Pp + Qq = R$ and set up the auxiliary system \[ \frac{dx}{P} = \frac{dy}{Q} = \frac{dz}{R}. \]
  • First compare $\dfrac{dy}{Q}$ and $\dfrac{dz}{R}$ to obtain one integral, then use it to simplify the remaining ratio.
problems.solution:
The given equation is \begin{equation}\label{eq:13Feb2026-1} (y^2 + z^2 - x^2)p - 2xy pq = -2xz. \end{equation} The auxiliary equations are \begin{equation}\label{eq:13Feb2026-2} \frac{dx}{y^2 + z^2 - x^2} = \frac{dy}{-2xy} = \frac{dz}{-2xz}. \end{equation}
Considering the last two fractions of \eqref{eq:13Feb2026-2}, we get \begin{align*} \frac{dy}{-2xy} = \frac{dz}{-2xz} & \implies \frac{dy}{y} = \frac{dz}{z} \\ & \implies \ln y = \ln z + \ln C_1 \\ & \implies \frac{y}{z} = C_1. \end{align*}
Using $\dfrac{y}{z} = C_1$, write $y = k z$. Then, \[ y^2 + z^2 = (k^2 + 1)z^2. \] Substituting this into \eqref{eq:13Feb2026-2} and considering the first two ratios, we get \begin{align*} \frac{dx}{(k^2 + 1)z^2 - x^2} = \frac{dz}{-2xz} \end{align*} This reduces to an exact differential and yields \[ \frac{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}{z} = C_2. \] The complete solution is therefore \[ F\!\left(\frac{y}{z}, \frac{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}{z}\right)=0, \] or equivalently, \[ \boxed{ \frac{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}{z} = \phi\!\left(\frac{y}{z}\right),} \] where $\phi$ is an arbitrary function.
Second method: After getting the first condition $\dfrac{y}{z} = C_1$, we could have also solved by choosing multipliers $x,y,z$ of \eqref{eq:13Feb2026-2}. This reduces to \begin{align*} \frac{x dx + y dy + z dz}{xy^2 + xz^2 - x^3 - 2xy^2 - 2xz^2} = \frac{x dx + y dy + z dz}{-x(x^2 + y^2 + z^2)} . \end{align*} Combining the third fraction of \eqref{eq:13Feb2026-2} with the above, we get \begin{align*} \frac{x dx + y dy + z dz}{-x(x^2 + y^2 + z^2)} = \frac{dz}{-2xz} & \implies \frac{2x dx + 2y dy + 2z dz}{-(x^2 + y^2 + z^2)} - \frac{dz}{z} = 0 \\ & \implies \ln (x^2 + y^2 + z^2) - \ln z = \ln C_2 \\ & \implies \left( \frac{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}{z} \right) = C_2. \end{align*} Thus, the solution is \[ F\!\left(\frac{y}{z}, \frac{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}{z}\right)=0. \]

problems.title: Solve y'' - 4y' + 4y = e^{2x}\sin x.
problems.problem:
Solve the differential equation
    \[
        y'' - 4y' + 4y = e^{2x}\sin x.
    \]
problems.hint:
• The characteristic equation has a repeated root $r=2$, so the homogeneous solution is $(c_1 + c_2 x)e^{2x}$. Because $e^{2x}$ appears in both the homogeneous solution and the right-hand side, multiply the usual undetermined-coefficients guess by $x^2$.
  • After substituting $y_p = x^2 e^{2x}(A \cos x + B \sin x)$ into the equation, collect coefficients of $e^{2x}\cos x$ and $e^{2x}\sin x$ separately and solve the resulting $2\times 2$ system for $A$ and $B$.
problems.solution:
The associated homogeneous equation is \[ y'' - 4y' + 4y = 0. \] Its characteristic equation is \[ r^2 - 4r + 4 = (r-2)^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r = 2 \quad \text{(multiplicity 2)}. \] Thus the general solution of the homogeneous equation is \[ y_h(x) = (c_1 + c_2 x) e^{2x}. \]
For a particular solution, since the nonhomogeneous term is $e^{2x} \sin x$ and $e^{2x}$ and $x e^{2x}$ are already in $y_h$, we try \[ y_p(x) = x^2 e^{2x} (A \cos x + B \sin x). \] Differentiate twice (using product rule repeatedly) and substitute into the original equation. After simplification and collecting like terms, the coefficients of $e^{2x} \cos x$ and $e^{2x} \sin x$ give the system \[ \begin{cases} -4A - 2B = 0, \\ 2A - 4B = 1. \end{cases} \] Solving yields $A = -\frac{1}{10}$ and $B = -\frac{2}{5}$. Therefore \[ y_p(x) = x^2 e^{2x} \left( -\frac{1}{10} \cos x - \frac{2}{5} \sin x \right) = -\frac{x^2 e^{2x}}{10} (\cos x + 4 \sin x). \]
The general solution is \[ y(x) = y_h(x) + y_p(x) = (c_1 + c_2 x) e^{2x} - \frac{x^2 e^{2x}}{10} (\cos x + 4 \sin x). \] Equivalently (by absorbing the constant term into $c_1$), \[ \boxed{y(x) = \bigl( c_1 + c_2 x - \sin x \bigr) e^{2x}.} \]

problems.title: Solve (x^2 - y^2 - z^2)p + 2xy q = 2xz,
problems.problem:
Solve 
    \[
        (x^2 - y^2 - z^2)p + 2xy q = 2xz,
    \]
    where $p = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} $ and $q = \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} $.
problems.hint:
• Use the last two proportions of the auxiliary equations to get the ratio involving $y$ and $z$ directly (leads to $y/z = c_1$).
  • Multiply the auxiliary equations by $x$, $y$, $z$ respectively and add to obtain a combination that gives $d(x^2 + y^2 + z^2)/ (x^2 + y^2 + z^2) = $ something involving $dz/z$; integrate to find the second relation.
problems.solution:
The given PDE is \[ (x^2 - y^2 - z^2)p + 2xy\, q = 2xz. \] Lagrange's auxiliary equations are \[ \frac{dx}{x^2 - y^2 - z^2} = \frac{dy}{2xy} = \frac{dz}{2xz}. \tag{1} \]
Taking the last two fractions: \[ \frac{dy}{2xy} = \frac{dz}{2xz} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dy}{y} = \frac{dz}{z}. \] Integrating, \[ \ln|y| = \ln|z| + \ln|c_1| \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{y}{z} = c_1. \tag{2} \] Now multiply the fractions of (1) by $x$, $y$, $z$ respectively and add: \[ x\,dx + y\,dy + z\,dz = \frac{x(x^2 - y^2 - z^2) + y(2xy) + z(2xz)}{-x(x^2 + y^2 + z^2)} \, dx \] \[ \frac{x\,dx + y\,dy + z\,dz}{-x(x^2 + y^2 + z^2)} = \frac{dz}{-2xz}. \] This simplifies to \[ \frac{d(x^2 + y^2 + z^2)}{x^2 + y^2 + z^2} = -\frac{dz}{z}. \] Integrating both sides: \[ \ln|x^2 + y^2 + z^2| = -\ln|z| + \ln|c_2| \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}{z} = c_2. \tag{3} \]
From (2) and (3), the general solution is an arbitrary relation between the two independent integrals: \[ \phi\left( \frac{y}{z},\ \frac{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}{z} \right) = 0, \] where $\phi$ is an arbitrary function.

problems.title: The functions $e^x, x e^x, x^2 e^x$ are linearly independent
problems.problem: Prove that the functions $e^x, x e^x, x^2 e^x$ are linearly independent. Hence form the differential equation whose roots are $e^x, x e^x, \text{ and } x^2 e^x$.
problems.hint:
• The functions will be linearly independent if the Wronskian is zero for some point in the domain which is $\mathbb{R} $.
  • For constructing the differential equation, note that \[ y(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{3} c_i y_i (x) \] will be a solution of the differential equation. Differentiate and eliminate the constants to get the differential equation.
problems.solution:
Let \[ y_1(x) = e^x, \quad y_2(x) = x e^x, \quad y_3(x) = x^2 e^x . \] Recall that the functions will linearly independent if the Wronskian is zero for some point $x \in \mathbb{R} $, which is the domain of the functions here. The Wronskian will be \begin{align*} W(y_1, y_2, y_3) & = \begin{vmatrix} y_1 & y_2 & y_3 \\[1ex] y_1' & y_2' & y_3' \\[1ex] y_1'' & y_2'' & y_3'' \\ \end{vmatrix} \\[2ex] & = \begin{vmatrix} e^x & x e^x & x^2 e^x \\[1ex] e^x & x e^x + e^x & x^2 e^x + 2x e^x \\[1ex] e^x & x e^x + 2 e^x & x^2 e^x + 4x e^x + 2e^x \\ \end{vmatrix} \\[2ex] & = 2e^{3x}. \end{align*} Note that $W(y_1, y_2, y_3) \neq 0$ for any $x \in \mathbb{R} $, but in particular, it is non zero for $x = 0$. So, the functions are linearly independent.
Now we need to construct an ODE whose solutions are $y_1, y_2, y_3$. This implies the general solution will be \[ y(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{3} c_i y_i (x), \] where $c_1, c_2, c_3$ are constants. Now to get the differential equation, we need to eliminate the constants. \begin{align*} y'(x) & = \sum_{i=1}^{3} c_i y_i'(x) = c_1 e^x + c_2 (x + 1)e^x + c_3 (x^2 + 2x)e^x \\ & = c_1 e^x + c_2 x e^x + c_3 x^2 e^x + c_2 e^x + 2c_3 x e^x \\ & = y(x) + c_2 e^x + c_3 2x e^x . \end{align*}
Now differentiating again, we get \begin{align*} y''(x) & = y'(x) + c_2 e^x + 2c_3x e^x + 2c_3 e^x \\ & = y'(x) + y'(x) - y(x) + 2c_3 e^x \\ & = 2 y'(x) - y(x) + 2c_3 e^x. \end{align*} Differentiating again, we get \begin{align*} y'''(x) & = 2y''(x) - y'(x) + 2c_3 e^x \\ & = 2 y''(x) - y'(x) + y''(x) - 2 y'(x) + y(x) \\ & = 3 y''(x) - 3 y'(x) + y(x). \end{align*} Thus, the differential equation will be \[ y''' - 3y'' + 3 y' - y = 0. \]

problems.title: Solve y(x + 4) (y + z) dx - x(y + 3z) dy + 2xy dz = 0.
problems.problem:
Solve
    \[
        y(x + 4) (y + z) \mathrm{d} x - x(y + 3z) \mathrm{d} y + 2xy \mathrm{d} z = 0.
    \]
problems.hint:
• Divide the equation by $xy(y+z)$ to get a more manageable form.
  • After simplification, the equation should reduce to a form where you can integrate both sides directly to find the solution.
problems.solution: The given differential equation is \begin{equation}\label{eq:13Mar2026-1} y(x + 4) (y + z) \mathrm{d} x - x(y + 3z) \mathrm{d} y + 2xy \mathrm{d} z = 0. \end{equation} Dividing by $xy(y+z)$ gives, \begin{align*} & \frac{x + 4}{x}\mathrm{d} x + \frac{2y \mathrm{d} z - y(y + 3z) \mathrm{d} y}{y(y+z)} = 0 \\ \implies & \frac{x + 4}{x}\mathrm{d} x + \frac{(2y \mathrm{d} z + 2y \mathrm{d} y) - 3(y+z) \mathrm{d} y}{y(y+z)} = 0 \\ \implies & \left( 1 + \frac{4}{x} \right) \mathrm{d} x + 2\left( \frac{\mathrm{d} z + \mathrm{d} y}{y+z} \right) - 3 \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{y} = 0 \\ \implies & \left( 1 + \frac{4}{x} \right) \mathrm{d} x + 2 \mathrm{d} \ln (y + z) - 3 \mathrm{d} \ln y = 0 \\ \implies & \left( 1 + \frac{4}{x} \right) \mathrm{d} x + \mathrm{d} \ln \left( \frac{(y + z)^2}{y^3} \right) = 0 \\ \implies & \int \left( 1 + \frac{4}{x} \right) \mathrm{d} x + \int \mathrm{d} \ln \left( \frac{(y + z)^2}{y^3} \right) = \ln c \\ \implies & x + 4 \ln x + \ln \left( \frac{(y + z)^2}{y^3} \right) = \ln c \\ \implies & -x = \ln \left( \frac{x^4 (y+z)^2}{cy^3} \right) \\ \implies & \frac{x^4 (y+z)^2}{cy^3} = e^{-x} \implies e^x x^4 (y+z)^2 = c y^3. \end{align*} Therefore, the solution of \eqref{eq:13Mar2026-1} is \[ \boxed{e^x x^4 (y+z)^2 = c y^3,} \] where $c$ is an arbitrary constant.

problems.title: Solve z = px + qy + p^2 + q^2,
problems.problem:
Find a complete integral of 
     \[
        z = px + qy + p^2 + q^2,
     \]
    where $p = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x}$ and $q = \frac{\partial z}{\partial y}$.
problems.hint: • Use Charpit's method to find the complete integral.
problems.solution:
The given PDE is \begin{equation}\label{eq:20Mar2026-1} z = px + qy + p^2 + q^2, \end{equation} where $p = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x}$ and $q = \frac{\partial z}{\partial y}$.
We will use Charpit's method to find the complete integral. Let \[ f(x,y,z,p,q) = z - px - qy - p^2 - q^2 \] Then, the Charpit's equations are \begin{equation}\label{eq:20Mar2026-2} \frac{\mathrm{d} p}{f_x + p f_z} = \frac{\mathrm{d} q}{f_y + q f_z} = \frac{\mathrm{d} z}{-p f_p - q f_q} = \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{-f_p} = \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{-f_q}. \end{equation} From \eqref{eq:20Mar2026-2}, we get \begin{align*} f_x + p f_z & = -p + p = 0, \\ f_y + q f_z & = -q + q = 0, \\ -p f_p - q f_q & = px + 2p + qy + 2q, \\ -f_p & = x + 2p, \\ -f_q & = y + 2q. \end{align*} Thus, the equation \eqref{eq:20Mar2026-2} reduce to \begin{equation}\label{eq:20Mar2026-3} \frac{\mathrm{d} p}{0} = \frac{\mathrm{d} q}{0} = \frac{\mathrm{d} z}{p(x + 2p) + q(y + 2q)} = \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{x + 2p} = \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{y + 2q}. \end{equation}
Taking the first and second fraction of \eqref{eq:20Mar2026-3}, we get $p = c_1$ and $q = c_2$, where $c_1, c_2$ are arbitrary constants. Substituting these into the remaining fractions of \eqref{eq:20Mar2026-1}, the required complete integral will be \[ z = c_1 x + c_2 y + c_1^2 + c_2^2, \] where $c_1, c_2$ are arbitrary constants.

problems.title: Solve the initial value problem \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{y^2 - x^2}{2xy}, y(1) = 2.
problems.problem:
Consider the initial value problem
    \[
        \frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x} = \frac{y^2 - x^2}{2xy}, \quad y(1) = 2.
    \]
    Find the explicit solution $y = y(x)$ and determine the largest interval containing $x = 1$ on which the solution exists.
problems.hint: • Try the substitution $v = \frac{y}{x}$, that is, $y = vx$. This transforms the equation into a separable ODE in terms of $v$ and $x$.
problems.solution:
The given initial value problem is \[ \frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x} = \frac{y^2 - x^2}{2xy}, \quad y(1) = 2. \] We will use the substitution $v = \frac{y}{x}$, that is, $y = vx$. Then, \[ \frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}x} = v + x \frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}x}. \] Substituting into the original equation gives \[ v + x \frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}x} = \frac{v^2 x^2 - x^2}{2 v x^2} = \frac{v^2 - 1}{2v}. \] Rearranging, we get \begin{align*} x \frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}x} & = \frac{v^2 - 1}{2v} - v \\ & = \frac{v^2 - 1 - 2v^2}{2v} \\ & = \frac{-v ^2 - 1}{2v} \\ & = -\frac{v^2 + 1}{2v}. \end{align*}
The above differential equation is a separable ODE. Separating variables gives \[ \frac{2v}{v^2 + 1} \mathrm{d}v = -\frac{\mathrm{d}x}{x}. \] Integrating both sides, we get \[ \ln(v^2 + 1) = -\ln \vert x \vert + \ln c, \] where $c$ is an arbitrary constant. This can be rewritten as \[ v^2 + 1 = \frac{c}{\vert x \vert}. \] Since we are looking for the solution around $x = 1$, we can take $x > 0$ and write \[ v^2 + 1 = \frac{c}{x}. \]
Substituting back $v = \frac{y}{x}$, we obtain \[ \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^2 + 1 = \frac{c}{x}, \] which simplifies to \[ y^2 + x^2 = cx. \] Using the initial condition $y(1) = 2$, we find $c = 5$. Therefore, the explicit solution is \[ y^2 + x^2 = 5x. \] Solving for $y$, we get \[ y = \pm \sqrt{5x - x^2}. \] Since $y(1) = 2 > 0$, we take the positive square root: \[ y = \sqrt{5x - x^2}. \]
The solution exists as long as the expression under the square root is non-negative, that is, \[ 5x - x^2 \geq 0 \quad \Rightarrow \ x(5 - x) \geq 0. \] This inequality holds for $0 \leq x \leq 5$. Since we are looking for the largest interval containing $x = 1$, the solution exists on the interval \[ \boxed{(0, 5)}. \]

problems.title: Differential Equation - Solve xzp + yzq = xy
problems.problem:
Find the general integral of 
    \[
        xzp + yzq = xy,
    \]
    where $p = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x}$ and $q = \frac{\partial z}{\partial y}$.
problems.hint: • Write the equation in Lagrange's form $Pp + Qq = R$ and choose appropriate multipliers to get the integrals.
problems.solution:
The given differential equation is \begin{equation}\label{eq:03Apr2026-1} xzp + yzq = xy, \end{equation} where $p = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x}$ and $q = \frac{\partial z}{\partial y}$.
The Lagrange's auxiliary equations are \begin{equation}\label{eq:03Apr2026-2} \frac{dx}{xz} = \frac{dy}{yz} = \frac{dz}{-xy}. \end{equation} From the first two fractions of \eqref{eq:03Apr2026-2}, we get \[ \frac{dx}{xz} = \frac{dy}{yz} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dx}{x} = \frac{dy}{y}. \] Integrating, we get \[ \ln|x| = \ln|y| + \ln|c_1| \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{x}{y} = c_1. \] Thus, the first integral is \begin{equation}\label{eq:03Apr2026-3} \frac{x}{y} = c_1 \end{equation}
Now to get the second integral, we can use the multipliers $(y, x, -2z)$. This gives \begin{align*} & \frac{y dx + x dy - 2z dz}{-2xyz} = \frac{dz}{-xy} \\ \implies & \frac{d(xy) - d(z^2)}{-2xyz} = \frac{dz}{-xy} \\ \implies & \frac{d(xy) - d(z^2)}{2z} = dz \\ \implies & d(xy) = d(z^2) \\ \implies & xy = z^2 + c_2. \end{align*} Thus, the second integral is \begin{equation}\label{eq:03Apr2026-4} xy = z^2 + c_2. \end{equation} From \eqref{eq:03Apr2026-3} and \eqref{eq:03Apr2026-4}, the general integral is \[ F\!\left( \frac{x}{y}, xy - z^2 \right) = 0, \] or equivalently, \[ \boxed{xy - z^2 = \phi\!\left( \frac{x}{y} \right),} \] where $\phi$ is an arbitrary function.
